title: Kim Jenkins: Building Vistra’s Next Chapter Around Execution
author: CFI.co Editorial
content_html:
[caption id="attachment_28551" align="alignright" width="300"] CEO: Kim Jenkins[/caption]

Vistra Group CEO Kim Jenkins is focused on three priorities: building a unified digital backbone, empowering market leaders, and developing AI literacy across the organisation. Together, they are intended to improve client experience, strengthen accountability, and unlock sustainable growth.

For Kim Jenkins, Vistra’s next phase is defined by execution.

The strategic direction is already clear. Vistra intends to become a digitally powered professional services leader. Jenkins’ role is to ensure that ambition is delivered consistently across markets.

Her priorities for the next 12 to 18 months are equally direct. Vistra must build a single digital backbone, simplify accountability around market leaders, and make every colleague AI-literate.

The appointment of Damian Leach as Chief AI and Digital Officer supports that agenda. So does the creation of a Digital Subcommittee of the Board. Together, they signal that digital transformation is a core strategic priority.

How Vistra Is Building a Unified Digital Backbone

The first priority is to create one digital backbone across the organisation.

Clients should experience one Vistra, regardless of whether they operate in London, Singapore, Hong Kong, or another market. Systems, workflows, and service standards must support that consistency.

Vistra Digital is central to this model. The platform allows clients to view their entity estate, upload documents, track requests, and communicate across markets.

It also gives clients greater control over how they engage. Some prefer digital self-service. Others want direct human interaction. Vistra is building both options into the same service model.

The company describes this as client choice rather than forced digitisation.

That distinction matters. Corporate and fund services remain complex, regulated, and highly dependent on judgement. Digital tools can improve speed and access, but they cannot replace professional assurance.

Jenkins’ view is that technology should strengthen the client relationship, not weaken it.

The benefits extend beyond the client interface. A unified digital backbone can also reduce internal friction and recover time lost to non-value work.

Vistra estimates that between 30 and 50 percent of employee time can be affected by fragmented processes. These include searching for files, chasing documents, and locating ownership information.

Reducing that burden allows colleagues to spend more time on advice, service quality, and client relationships.

How Market Leaders Improve the Client Experience

The second priority is structural simplification.

Jenkins has reorganised the business around empowered market leaders. These leaders hold responsibility for sales, account management, and service delivery.

The purpose is to create one clear point of accountability for each client relationship.

This structure is important because seamless service begins with people. Technology cannot correct an operating model that is unclear or fragmented.

Market leaders sit closest to clients. They understand local regulation, commercial practice, language, and culture.

The wider organisation is therefore designed to support them.

This model also protects local expertise while improving global consistency. Clients receive the same professional standard across markets, but still benefit from advisers who understand local conditions.

Jenkins sees this as the foundation of a stronger client experience.

A client may need a document while travelling across time zones. Another may require guidance on a complex compliance issue.

Digital access can solve the first problem. Trusted professional judgement remains essential for the second.

Vistra’s structure is intended to bring both together.

How Kim Jenkins Leads Transformation

Jenkins places strong emphasis on simplification and ownership.

Large organisations often struggle because accountability is divided. Decisions slow down, responsibilities overlap, and teams spend too much time coordinating internally.

The market leader model is designed to reduce that friction.

Communication is another priority. Jenkins believes transformation works only when colleagues feel heard, not simply informed.

Employees must be able to question decisions, identify problems, and explain what is happening close to the client. That feedback allows the organisation to adjust more quickly.

Implementation also receives close attention.

Vistra is rolling out change market by market, supported by dedicated local teams. These teams remain in place long enough to embed new processes.

The approach is deliberate. Large-scale programmes often fail because too much attention is given to strategy and too little to delivery.

Jenkins is seeking to correct that imbalance.

How Vistra Is Building AI Literacy

The third priority is to make AI literacy a company-wide capability.

Vistra’s first 250 AI users came from the leadership team. The intention was to ensure that senior leaders understood the tools before asking others to adopt them.

More than 1,100 colleagues later volunteered to become AI champions.

That level of participation suggests that employees see AI as an opportunity rather than simply a threat.

AI is already being used across Vistra’s services. It supports research, regulatory scanning, onboarding, entity management, payroll, and compliance workflows.

Geni, Vistra’s AI assistant, helps clients access information and accelerate service requests. Clients can use self-service tools or work directly with specialists.

Behind the scenes, AI helps advisers process information and monitor regulatory change more efficiently.

The objective is not to remove human judgement. It is to improve the speed and quality of preparation.

Where Human Judgement Remains Essential

Jenkins draws a clear boundary around the role of AI.

Technology can prepare information, identify patterns, and reduce administrative work. It cannot replace professional responsibility.

Clients want confidence that their entities are compliant and in good standing. They also want access to someone who understands the relevant regulations.

This is especially important in high-stakes governance and compliance matters. Errors can lead to financial penalties, reputational damage, and operational disruption.

Vistra’s position is straightforward: technology without expertise creates risk, while expertise without technology creates inefficiency.

Its model combines both.

How Vistra Preserves Entrepreneurial Culture

Vistra has completed 18 acquisitions over 15 years. That growth has created scale, but it also creates integration risk.

Large organisations can lose the speed and ownership that helped them grow.

Jenkins is seeking to preserve those qualities through local accountability. Market leaders retain commercial responsibility while operating within a global framework.

Governance is also being strengthened without creating unnecessary bureaucracy.

The Digital Subcommittee of the Board provides oversight. Execution remains with teams operating in local markets.

Innovation is encouraged from both directions. Strategic priorities come from the top, while ideas also emerge through the AI champions programme.

The result should be a company that is more consistent without becoming rigid.

What Vistra’s Next Phase Means for Stakeholders

For clients, the next phase means a more seamless and accessible service.

For colleagues, it means clearer processes, better tools, and less time spent on fragmented work.

For shareholders, it means stronger retention, operational leverage, and further geographic expansion.

These outcomes are closely connected.

A unified digital platform improves service. Clearer accountability strengthens execution. AI literacy increases productivity and supports better decisions.

Together, they form the operating model Jenkins is seeking to build.

Her ambition is not simply to digitise professional services. It is to create a global organisation that combines scale with local expertise and technology with judgement.

The principle is simple: clients choose how they work, and Vistra’s experts handle the rest.
